notes.anchor: what he will need can be transferred by exchange at Athens
notes.note: Permutatio was the banking practice of supplying funds in a distant city by a paper transfer through agents, sparing the risk of shipping coin. Cicero is arranging his son Marcus's allowance for his studies at Athens this way rather than sending cash overland.

notes.anchor: Publius Crassus, Venuleia's son, died while his father Publius Crassus the ex-consul was still alive
notes.note: Another exemplum gathered for the Consolatio --- a parent who survived a grown child. The exact identity of this P. Crassus and his mother Venuleia is uncertain and unbound; Cicero himself is unsure of the facts and asks Atticus to verify.
A further parent-outliving-child case for the Consolatio: M. Aemilius Lepidus Regillus, who predeceased his father. Neither Regillus nor the relevant Lepidus is bound to an entity in this corpus.

notes.anchor: and the Precius one as well
notes.note: Precius (or Praecius) had left Cicero a legacy; the 'Precius business' is the winding-up of that inheritance, which Atticus handled as Cicero's man of affairs. The parallel 'Cispius business' is a similar private matter that has no entity in this corpus.
